title: Gentrification and Racial Distrust in Communities: Evidence from 911 Calls
abstract: The prevalence of racial bias in policing has long concerned social scientists and policymakers. This article studies a predecessor mechanism that constitutes an important source of policing bias in American society: calls by individuals to the police to investigate “suspicious” behaviors, often involving neighbors. We construct a novel data set of more than 39 million 911 calls across 14 U.S. cities from 2011 to 2020. These data, obtained through the digitization initiatives of local governments, provide us with a unique opportunity to study neighborhood-level trust and social cohesion and demonstrate how changes to a neighborhood’s composition lead to systematic increases in the prevalence of “unfounded” suspicion calls to the police. Across a range of specifications, the proportion of unfounded suspicion calls increases as more non-Black residents move into neighborhoods with historically high levels of Black residents. This relationship is exacerbated in gentrifying neighborhoods and those with public spaces that enable more contact between community members. However, we also find some evidence that Black leadership and public support of Black citizens in communities mitigate the association between non-Black residents and the proportion of unfounded 911 calls. We discuss our results and implications for future research and policy.

author.Uttara M. Ananthakrishnan: Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania 15213;
author.Sharique Hasan: Duke University, Fuqua School of Business, Durham, North Carolina 27708;
author.Anuj Kumar: University of Florida, Warrington College of Business, Gainesville, Florida 32611

article.1. Introduction: In recent years, two phenomena have significantly impacted the lives of Black Americans. First is the prevalence of racial bias in policing in the United States (Baumgartner et al. 2018). The desire to address this issue has only increased in recent years as the emergence of the Black Lives Matter (BLM) movement and the national outrage over the George Floyd killing have highlighted the widespread police violence faced by Black Americans. A second phenomenon is an experience referred to in the media as “Living While Black” (Dias et al. 2018,Fieldstadt 2018,Griggs 2018,Holson 2018). Neighbors and business owners disproportionately call the police on Black citizens whom they perceive as “suspicious,” even though their actions are anything but (e.g., mowing their lawn, having a barbeque, listening to music). Indeed, a recent Pew Research survey found that 65% of Black adults say they have been in situations where they were perceived as suspicious because of their ethnicity. In contrast, only 25% of White adults were (PewResearch 2019). The latter phenomenon arises in mundane situations in communities, particularly in gentrifying neighborhoods, where interracial interaction has increased. Indeed, the repeated casual use of the police against Black individuals suggests a persistent racial distrust and highlights another possible mechanism driving bias. Yet despite the growing reports of the biased use of police against Black community members by their neighbors, rigorous evidence for this phenomenon and the mechanisms driving it remains limited (Henderson and Jefferson-Jones 2019). This paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding when we should expect the biased use of police in communities and how society might mitigate this effect. Specifically, we build on several strands of theory in the social sciences, namelycontact theoryandracial threat theory. Racial threat theory, for instance, argues that community-driven bias in police use against Black citizens is most likely in situations where there is significant interracial contact, especially among groups that also differ in socioeconomic status. Such situations are especially likely in communities where gentrification—the populating of once segregated and low-income areas by the affluent—is unfolding. Furthermore, based on the predictions of contact theory, we also argue that communities may mitigate this bias through leadership that reflects and supports the minority community. To test our predictions, we constructed a novel data set comprising more than 39 million 911 calls across 14 U.S. cities and more than 17,000 census tracts from 2011 to 2020. These call data were obtained through the digitization initiatives of local governments, providing detailed information about calls to the police and their outcomes. Specifically, we are interested in calls reporting “suspicious” behaviors in neighborhoods, particularly those that were ultimately classified as “unfounded” or without merit after investigation. We combine this unique data source with detailed information about demographic changes in the represented census tracts. Additionally, we integrate data on political change and public support expressed by those in power (e.g., on Twitter) for Black citizens. Using this combined data set, we examine whether the distribution of “unfounded suspicion-related” calls varies geographically and over time. In other words, we investigate whether Black communities undergoing gentrification are more likely to experience an increase in such calls and whether public support mitigates this effect. Across a series of specifications, we find evidence for an increase in “unfounded suspicion-related” calls associated with an influx of non-Black residents into communities in our sample. This relationship, however, is not uniform. Using classifications of “gentrifiable” neighborhoods based on the urban and regional planning literature, we find that the above relationship is most substantial in neighborhoods located in inner cities that also had low levels of housing development after the 1980s, relative to nearby communities. Such gentrifiable communities tended to be low-income and housed historically Black residents. Furthermore, supporting the theory that interracial contact is a mechanism driving the increase in unfounded suspicion-related calls, we find that such calls are more common in gentrifiable communities with more green space, where interaction is more likely. Finally, we also see evidence that Black leadership and public support for Black citizens may help dampen this effect. These findings advance our understanding of the impact of gentrification on communities of color and provide evidence of the biased use of police within these communities. Our work has several implications for both research and policy. In terms of contributions to the literature, we see two primary ones. The first is phenomenological; we provide preliminary evidence that in the study of bias in policing, we should consider not justpolice biasbut the biaseduseof police by communities. Second, we also provide evidence in a nonlaboratory setting for the predictions of contact and racial threat theories. We find evidence of mechanisms from each of these theories operating in the data, with racial threat theory finding support in our main effects and some predictions of contact theory in the situations where bias is minimized or mitigated.
article.2. Theoretical Development: Gentrificati: In recent years, two phenomena have had an indelible impact on the lives of Black Americans. The first, as evidenced by the social movements focused on racial justice over the past several years, is the presence of significant bias in policing that Black citizens face. The second is the gentrification and reorganization of the once primarily Black communities by affluent, primarily White implants who have sought out inner city neighborhoods for their lower cost of living, proximity to city centers, and growing amenities fueled by the investment cycle and migration. Although these two phenomena have been studied separately in disparate literature, increasing qualitative evidence suggests that gentrification and the policing of Black citizens may be meaningfully related. 2.1. Biased Policing in America Regarding police bias, a large amount of literature documents the prevalence of racial disparities in policing in the United States. In particular, researchers find that Black citizens are significantly more likely to be the target of police activity (Anwar and Fang 2006,Gelman et al. 2007,Antonovics and Knight 2009,Epp et al. 2014,Baumgartner et al. 2018). Black and Hispanic drivers, for instance, are more likely to be targeted, stopped, and searched by police relative to White drivers (Pierson et al. 2020). In this body of work, most authors theorize that such disparities emanate from biased police officers, who are statistically more likely to target Black rather than White citizens, all else being equal (Anwar and Fang 2006). As such, theory and practice have honed in on police organizations and officers as the primary institutions of concern in addressing these large-scale racial disparities in policing. 2.2. Community-Driven Bias in Policing Although evidence of police bias from these studies is compelling, the data primarily reflect police activity in one context: on the road, where police have significant discretion in who to target. However, significant disparities in policing also exist in the broader community context, including businesses, neighborhoods, and public spaces. Although bias in these settings has received much less attention in the academic literature (Quillian and Pager 2001,Kefalas 2003,Sampson and Raudenbush 2004), media reports and qualitative reports suggest that it is no less prevalent (Solnit 2016,Fayyad 2017,Dias et al. 2018,Holson 2018,Streeter 2020). Indeed, the differential targeting of Black citizens by police—in their neighborhoods, homes, and larger communities—has become a topic of significant concern in society. For example, in 2009, Henry Louis Gates, Jr., a distinguished professor at Harvard University, was arrested entering his own home in Cambridge, MA. More recently, a Black man, George Floyd, was murdered by a police officer after a clerk reported him to the police for ostensibly using a counterfeit bill, sparking a national social movement against bias in policing. In both of these instances, community members—in one case a neighbor and another a business owner—were the reason police were called to the scene. These examples suggest that a predecessor mechanism may exist, racial mistrust from community members and neighbors, leading to biased policing. Yet despite significant media reports and a long history of research on racial dynamics within American communities, studying the role of community members in perpetuating bias in policing has remained challenging. 2.3. Rising Gentrification in America and Interracial Contact America’s communities have long been flashpoints in its struggle with bias. Much of the prior literature has viewed the instantiation of bias as a movement toward greater segregation, with the Black and White population living in different social spheres and having few interpersonal interactions in these spheres (Clark 1986,Sampson 2009,Shertzer and Walsh 2019). However, researchers have recently documented a decline in segregation in America’s urban core. For instance, work byEllen et al. (2012)andLogan and Zhang (2010)documented that integrated neighborhoods in metropolitan tracts have increased from 20% to 30% between 1990 and 2010. This decline in segregation and the corresponding increase in contact between different groups does not necessarily mean a decline in bias against minority groups. Indeed, prior theory leads to mixed predictions about the enactment of bias because of neighborhood integration. Do incidents like those described in the media—where community members use the police as an intermediary to enact racial bias against their neighbors—constitute the norm? Or are these examples the exception to more amicable integration? 2.3.1. Contact Theory vs. Racial Threat Theory. Prior theories provide no single prediction of the likely consequence of greater integration on using the police to enact bias. On the one hand, contact theory (Allport et al. 1954,Pettigrew and Tropp 2005,Pettigrew et al. 2011) posits that contact—that is, the interaction between individuals from different racial and ethnic groups—may be a catalyst for decreasing negative racial attitudes. The theory postulates that the mechanism driving prejudice is “irrationality” or the holding of incorrect beliefs about other racial or ethnic groups. Exposure to members of another race can change prior beliefs on stereotypes, leading to increased harmony among different races (Jackman and Crane 1986,Sigelman and Welch 1993,Ellison and Powers 1994,Hood and Morris 1998,Emerson et al. 2002). On the other hand, racial threat theory (Blalock 1967) predicts otherwise. This theory begins with a different starting mechanism—the desire to maintain power by a dominant social group. Racial threat theory argues that the presence of minorities in the neighborhood can increase conflict between the members of the dominant group and the other group. For example, researchers have found that an increase in the Black population is associated with lower racial tolerance among the White population, who perceive this increase as a threat to their power (Glaser 1994,Sharp and Joslyn 2008). Empirically, however, both theories have received, at best, mixed support in the literature. Researchers studying the predictions of contact theory in various settings—from laboratories to surveys—have found that increased contact may decrease conflict and competition in some contexts but increase it in others (McClain et al. 2006,Sharp and Joslyn 2008). Racial threat theory has also received mixed empirical support. Some studies find evidence of decreased tolerance from interracial contact (Giles and Buckner 1993,Glaser 1994,Longoria Jr 1996), whereas other studies find only mixed evidence (McClain et al. 2006). Notwithstanding the mixed predictions of thecontactandracial threattheories on the direction of change in bias, empirical research consistently found evidence of a persistent bias against Black community members. The research shows significant amounts of implicit and explicit negative associations toward Black Americans (Bobo and Massagli 2001,De Houwer et al. 2009). Indeed, scholars have found that stereotyping and automatic and implicit bias exist even when individuals consciously reject racism (O’Brien et al. 2010). These biases are brought to the fore when there is frequent contact between groups because of gentrification. Incoming residents associate their perception of crime and disorder in the gentrified neighborhood with the existing Black population.Kefalas (2003)showed through interviews that the urge to maintain order in a neighborhood is a proxy for feeling threatened by Black citizens in urban neighborhoods.Sampson and Raudenbush (2004)argued that White Americans associate Black Americans with crime, violence, and disorder because of cultural stereotyping (Devine and Elliot 1995). Furthermore,Quillian and Pager (2001)used neighborhood level and individual surveys in three major cities in the United States and demonstrated a positive correlation between the percentage of young Black residents and perceptions of crime in a neighborhood controlling for crime rates. In doing so,Quillian and Pager (2001)argued that White Americans systematically overestimate crime in areas with a higher proportion of Black residents. Furthermore, this perceptual bias drives residential segregation and explains why White Americans often stigmatize Black citizen majority neighborhoods. The perceptual bias of incoming affluent residents to the gentrified neighborhoods can manifest as social control through a proactive form of policing called “order maintenance policing” (Sharp 2014). Order maintenance policing differs from other kinds of policing because it emphasizes the role of citizen involvement in policing and proactive efforts to reduce perceived disorders in urban neighborhoods by the members of the neighborhood (Skogan 2006). If there is a positive correlation between the perception of disorder and the Black population of a neighborhood without an actual increase in crime (Quillian 1996,Sampson and Raudenbush 2004), it indicates the use of order maintenance policing to target the Black population. In our context, this would result in increased 911 calls to the police reporting suspicious behavior when there is actually no threat or disorder. Overall, the empirical evidence suggests that such bias is quite durable. Increased interaction often leads to further entrenchment of biased beliefs about the minority community. A critical theoretical question, however, is whether these biased beliefs—which may persist or get exacerbated through interaction—lead to biased behaviors. In the context of policing, an implication of racial threat theory is that as more non-Black individuals move into primarily Black residents’ neighborhoods, their bias will either persist or strengthen. Furthermore, their biased beliefs and desire to maintain social order and their dominant position would make them more likely to use the police as a mechanism for control against their Black neighbors. In-migration of non-Black individuals in a neighborhood is associated with increased biased use of police. 2.4. Moderating the Link Between Gentrification and Biased Use of Police 2.4.1. Gentrifiable Neighborhoods. Research in sociology has found that greater social, cultural, and economic distance between groups leads to more distrust and potentially more conflict. Consequently, most geographic sorting over the contemporary history of the United States has tended toward greater community homogeneity, with segregated neighborhoods that facilitate little contact between different races and classes (Clark 1986,Orfield et al. 1994,Moody 2001). Such segregation maintains geographic and social segregation between races and classes and reduces the possibility of contact between diverse groups. However, economists and sociologists have recently documented an increase in the “gentrification” of previously neglected and once-segregated areas in urban centers (Ellen et al. 2012). As gentrification progresses, more affluent and White individuals and families move into formerly disinvested, working-class, or vacant neighborhoods, leading to significant reinvestment and rising home prices (Glass 1964,Lees 2003,Ellen et al. 2012,Lees et al. 2013). Moreover, local governments, urban planners, and developers also play a significant role in reinvesting in these formerly segregated communities. Further investment in cultural and commercial amenities signals a positive change in a neighborhood’s desirability for the affluent (Florida 2002,2005). These transformations have also led to a change in attitudes about living in integrated neighborhoods among the White population (Bruch and Mare 2006), further increasing racial and class mixing in once formerly segregated—mostly minority-dominated—neighborhoods (Donegan et al. 2008,Sharp and Joslyn 2008,Zuk et al. 2015). Unlike most other communities, these formerly segregated potential mobility targets for the affluent are the “gentrifiable” neighborhoods with a significant potential for interracial and interclass contact. How does this increasing gentrification impact interracial and interclass contact in these previously segregated and neglected communities? Prior theory suggests that a dominant social group may perceive “disorder” in their new communities (Wilson and Kelling 1982,Kelling and Coles 1997), especially when there is a considerable racial and class difference between themselves and original members of the community. Research suggests that even when actual disorder in a neighborhood is minimal, racial stigma and implicit biases may cause individuals from the dominant racial or class group to perceive high rates of disorder, crime, and a lack of safety in their surroundings (Sampson and Raudenbush 2004). As a result, the dominant group should be more likely to use institutional means—such as the police—to protect their interests and safety in what they perceive to be a hostile environment in their new community. This dynamic is more likely to play out in contexts of increasing gentrification and significant class and racial differences between newly arriving individuals and existing community members. In-migration of non-Black individuals is associated with increased biased use of police in “gentrifiable” neighborhoods. 2.4.2. More Interracial Contact. The arguments above articulate the broader social context in which we should see more “unfounded suspicion”: when there is more contact between individuals from different groups, especially in gentrifying communities. According to the predictions ofracial threat theory, increasing contact should lead to more suspicion. And in our context, this suspicion should translate into a rise in the biased use of police to maintain social order by the dominant group. However, the precondition for the operation of this mechanism is contact, not just proximity. Sociological research has shown that even within smaller-scale urban centers, individuals may continue to live relatively segregated lives (Grannis 1998). Thus, gentrification may not necessarily imply contact if individuals self-segregate into micro-geographies within the larger urban context. However, even in cities with segregated micro-geographies, public green spaces serve as a venue for contact between individuals from many backgrounds. If the mechanisms described above are operative, then we should expect that the effect of gentrification on unfounded suspicion will be more significant in communities with more public green space, where more interracial contact is likely to occur. In-migration of non-Black residents is associated with increased biased use of police in “gentrifiable” neighborhoods with more green space. 2.5. Mitigating Unfounded Suspicion Through Political Leadership Existing research suggests that there may be potential solutions to mitigate the impact of these biases. Contact theory, for example, proposes that mere contact alone might not be enough to alleviate distrust and bias. However, when contact is combined with shared goals, it can help reduce preexisting biases and enable more productive interactions (Allport et al. 1954,Sharp and Joslyn 2008,Sharp 2014). Studies indicate that minority representation in elected government can improve negative racial attitudes. When White individuals observe that their interests are not harmed under non-White leadership, negative stereotypes and fear tend to diminish (Hajnal 2001). In addition to the impact of having a Black elected official, the existing theory also postulates that active encouragement of harmony and support for marginalized groups by those in power can help reduce bias and prejudice. Today, social media platforms in general and Twitter in particular have changed how those in power communicate with the public (Lassen and Brown 2011,Towner and Dulio 2012,Jungherr 2014,Campante et al. 2018). We postulate that when those in power explicitly support Black Americans, for example, tweeting support during Black History Month, celebrating Juneteenth, and supporting the BLM movement, we should expect lower levels of unfounded suspicion calls because of gentrification. As a consequence, we expect the following patterns. The association between non-Black residents’ in-migration and biased use of police is lower when the city has a non-White elected official. The association between non-Black residents’ in-migration and biased use of police is lower when elected officials express public support for Black Americans. A summary of the hypotheses, theoretical constructs used, and the variables used to capture the theoretical constructs are provided inTable 1. article.3. Data Description and Variable Constru: Below we describe the construction of the data set used to test our hypotheses. We conduct our analyses by combining four primary sources of information. The first is a database of more than 39 million 911 calls collected through the administrative processes of 14 cities across the United States between 2011 and 2020—a decade. With these data, we construct our primary dependent variables: (a) the frequency of 911 calls reporting “suspicious behavior” and (b) the likelihood that the police department eventually classifies such a call as “unfounded.” We aggregate these data into year-census tract-level observations. Next, we merge demographic and other community data from the American Community Survey (ACS) to analyze whether changes in racial and ethnic composition affect the extent of unfounded suspicion calls in the census tracts in our sample. Third, we collect data on political change, the election of mayors from a minority background. Finally, we collect data from Twitter that allows us to capture public support for Black lives by other local elected officials representing the census tracts in our data. 3.1. 911 Call Data from Computer Aided Dispatch System We derive our 911 call data from the Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) systems used by police departments in 14 major U.S. cities, including Charleston, Cincinnati, Durham, Ft. Lauderdale, Las Vegas, Nashville, New Orleans, Orlando, Phoenix, San Diego, San Francisco, Santa Monica, Seattle, and Virginia Beach. We show the location of the cities in our analysis inFigure 1. We can observe that these cities cover many geographical regions in the United States with different political leanings.1 Many major cities across the United States make these data publicly available through their open data portals. Because the data come from the administrative process of police and other emergency service dispatches, each observation reflects four primary steps. These include the following: Call Initiation—A citizen calls 911 for assistance. Call Categorization—The telephone dispatcher receiving the 911 call classifies it using one of the predetermined categories. Call Disposition—The dispatcher assigns police officers to attend to the incident reported in the 911 call. The officer(s) visit(s) the scene of the 911 call and take(s) necessary actions to resolve the incident. Upon resolution, the officer assigns a disposition code to the 911 call. Call Recording—The CAD system registers this information and periodically updates the details on the city’s open data portal. Although most CAD systems include the information above, there is still meaningful heterogeneity in how a given police department categorizes each call. Thefirstlevel of heterogeneity in our data comes from how dispatchers categorize the 911 calls based on a list of predefined categories. The categorization varies depending on police department policy and state and city laws. The call types are detailed to increase operational efficiency and direct the appropriate law enforcement units. Given this heterogeneity, we need a standard classification for the different 911 call types to infer specific behavioral patterns across cities. We follow the Vera Institute of Justice in its analysis of 911 calls in four cities in the United States (https://www.vera.org/). Specifically, the Vera Institute categorizes each call type to fit a common call category. For example, it combines more than 500 call types into several broad call categories, such as “Accidents/Traffic Safety,” “Alarms,” “Behavioral Health,” “Domestic Violence,” “Drugs,” “Property Crimes,” “Sex Offenses,” “Violent Crimes,” and “Warrants.” We are particularly interested in suspicion-related 911 calls. Vera classifies these calls as “Suspicion,” “Disturbance & Environment-Related,” and “Trespasser & Prowler” categories. We classify each 911 call in our data as either one of the suspicion-related categories or “other” (if they do not fall under these suspicious categories). We also remove animal-related call types that could be part of the “Disturbance & Environment-Related” category. We replicate the Vera Institute’s classification for all of the cities in our data using this procedure.Figure 2represents the distribution of different types of 911 calls.2 Thesecondlevel of heterogeneity in 911 calls comes from how attending officers resolve each complaint. This resolution process is called “disposition.” We again follow the Vera Institute’s taxonomy in standardizing call dispositions across cities. There can be broad categories of call dispositions, such as “Citation Issued,” “Enforcement Activity,” “Report Generated,” “Non-Arrest Activity,” “Cancellation,” and “False Alarm.” We are specifically interested in dispositions when the police officers either did not find any violations or found a violation that requires no action in the complaint made in the 911 call. Vera codes these situations as “Unfounded” and “Warning/No Report Activity/Resolved on Scene” dispositions. We call the suspicious calls with unfounded disposition as “Unfounded suspicious 911 calls.”Figure 3demonstrates the percentage of unfounded calls across different call categories.3 Finally, thethirdsource of heterogeneity in 911 calls comes from how different police departments share these data on their city’s open data portals. This information is available via the Police Data Initiative (a consortium of more than 100 law enforcement agencies), The Police Foundation, and the Office of Community Oriented Policing Services (a component of the U.S. Department of Justice responsible for advancing the practice of community policing through information and grant resources). Although we found 34 cities hosting these data on their city’s open data portals, there was significant variation in the completeness and quality of data. Specifically, our analysis required data on the “final disposition” to differentiate between legitimate threats to the public versus unwarranted calls made out of mistrust and suspicion. Fourteen of these 34 cities we evaluated contained complete information between 2011 and 2020. We use the complete sample of 14 cities in our analysis. 3.1.1. Mapping 911 Calls to Census Tracts. In our data, a cross street (e.g., Main St. and 1st St.) identifies the incident’s location for a 911 call. We assign each call to a census tract (hereafter referred to as CT) using this information. Next, we aggregate the number of calls by type at the CT and year levels. Upon aggregation, we obtain the total annual calls of different types, totaling 17,617 CT-year observations for each call and disposition type.4 Table 2reports the statistics of different types of 911 calls. On average, 14.5% of the 911 calls were suspicion related, and the remaining 85.5% were related to other issues. Table 2.Summary Statistics of Suspicion-Related 911 Calls Table 2.Summary Statistics of Suspicion-Related 911 Calls However, different police departments differ in classifying different types of 911 calls and their dispositions. As indicated in the previous section, we used the classification of the call types and their disposition by the Vera Institute of Justice (https://www.vera.org/). It is vital to account for the city-level differences in the classification of calls and their dispositions. We do this by using the CT-level fixed effects in our econometric analysis. 3.1.2. Unfounded 911 Call Dependent Variables. The theoretical motivation behind these two measures is the qualitative evidence that suggests that members of the majority community call the police to report suspicious behavior of Black individuals in their neighborhood. Thus, the percentage of suspicion-related 911 calls measures the distrust in a neighborhood. Moreover, the percentage of suspicion-related calls where the attending police officers found no violation (an “unfounded” disposition) indicates the unfounded distrust or presence of bias in using the police against neighbors. Therefore, the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls is our measure of racial bias in the neighborhood.5In our data, 20% of the total suspicion-related 911 calls were unfounded. 3.2. Census Tract Socioeconomic Variables To testHypotheses 1and2, we need information on the demographic composition and change at the census tract level. We collected these data from the American Community Survey (ACS) from 2011 to 2020. The five-year ACS estimates provide detailed social and demographic variables in CTs we use in our analyses. Specifically, we constructed the following variables: ACS Population Tables: The Black and non-Black population in each CT from 2011 to 2020 from the ACS Population Tables. ACS Income Tables: Number of Black and non-Black households earning <$50,000, $50,000–150,000, and >$150,000 and their median household income in each CT from 2011 to 2020. ACS Educational Attainment Tables: Black and non-Black population above 25 years of age with and without a college degree in each CT from 2011 to 2020. ACS Household Characteristics Tables: The number of occupied and unoccupied housing units, the median value of housing units, and the vintage of housing units (e.g., number of housing units built between 1960 and 1969, 1970 and 1979, 1980 and 1989, etc.). Thus, we collected detailed socioeconomic, demographic, and housing characteristics data at the CT level for 17,617 CT-year observations in the United States from 2011 to 2020, representing the span of our 911 call data. We present summary statistics of the variables used inTable 3. Table 3.Summary Statistics of Socioeconomic Variables Table 3.Summary Statistics of Socioeconomic Variables We use this information to verify that our 14 cities are comparable in important aspects to other large U.S. cities. The Core-Based Statistical Areas (CBSA)6for the 14 cities in our data are ranked between 2 to 104 in the top most populous CBSAs in the United States. Therefore, we compare the CT-level average socioeconomic variables for the CBSAs of our 14 cities with the top 150 most populous CBSAs in the United States.Table 4shows the comparison. Table 4.Comparisons of CBSAs in Our Sample with the Top 150 Most Populous CBSAs in the Unites States Table 4.Comparisons of CBSAs in Our Sample with the Top 150 Most Populous CBSAs in the Unites States The CTs in our data are broadly similar to the CTs of other populous CBSAs in the United States. Specifically, the low-income Black population and fewer number of Black residents with a college degree in our CTs is similar to CTs in other high-population cities.7 3.3. Classifying Census Tracts as Gentrifiable Hypotheses 2and3postulate that the effect of interracial interaction on unfounded suspicion-related calls is heightened in “gentrifiable” neighborhoods and those with more green space. We build on prior literature to develop indicators for these constructs at the CT level. Research in sociology on gentrification indicates that areas in inner cities with older housing stock are prime candidates for gentrification. For instance,Freeman (2005)proposed the following two conditions for a CT to have a high future likelihood of gentrification; (a) it should be located in the inner (central) city, and (b) the number of housing units built after 1980 in a CT should be lower than the median value in its CBSA. To do so, we categorized a CT to be in the inner (central) city based on data from the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) on designated central city neighborhoods by (Hollar and Usowski 2007). According to the HUD, these CTs are called Low-Income Housing Tax Credit Qualified Census Tracts. These CTs have a poverty rate of at least 25%, or 50% of their households have incomes below 60% of the median income in that area. We computed the number of housing units built between 1980 and 2010 for each CT from the ACS housing characteristics tables data. Then, we identified CTs with lower than the median value of housing stocks built post-1980 in their CBSA. We called the CTs fulfilling both requirements “gentrifiable” CTs in 2011. We present summary statistics inTable 5. Table 5.Differences Between Gentrifiable CTs and Non-Gentrifiable CTs Table 5.Differences Between Gentrifiable CTs and Non-Gentrifiable CTs To validate this measure of a gentrifiable census tract, we compare the summary statistics of race-based socioeconomic variables in the gentrifiable CTs to understand the potential for racial mixing in such areas inTable 5. Prior research indicates that the gentrification of an area often results in a wealthier White population moving into predominantly low-income Black neighborhoods. This comparison suggests meaningful qualitative differences in tracts classified based on the likelihood of future gentrification. First, the gentrifiable CTs have a significantly higher Black population than non-gentrifiable CTs (the ratio of Black to total population was 954/3,406 in gentrifiable versus 466/4,447 in non-gentrifiable CTs). Second, this population is likely to come from the lowest individual income segment (the ratio of low-income Black households to total households was 273/1,064 in gentrifiable versus 96/1,615 in non-gentrifiable CTs). Finally, residents in these tracts dwell in lower-value homes. These findings align with the demographic factors that predict a higher potential for gentrification in the prior literature (Freeman 2005,Zuk et al. 2015). Most importantly, the above comparison shows that as gentrification unfolds, more interracial and inter-status contact will occur. 3.4. Green Space Data Prior research suggests that proximity alone may not lead to contact (Bickford and Massey 1991,Grannis 1998,Tach 2014,Chaskin and Joseph 2015), because individuals in the same tract may segregate into micro-geographies. However, prior literature suggests that communities with more green space—for example, public parks, walkways, etc.—have more community interaction through activities such as walking, running, or playing, among other benefits (Kaiser et al. 2013,Hartig et al. 2014,Larson et al. 2018,Acevedo-Garcia et al. 2020,Beck et al. 2020). However, as we have theorized, this increased interaction may, paradoxically, lead to opportunities for racial prejudice and conflict. Following past research, we use data on green spaces from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) (Jia et al. 2021).8These data are based on the National Land Cover Database (NLCD) and use satellite imagery to calculate the green space available in each census tract. Specifically, the CDC releases the percentage of impervious surface areas at the census tract level. An increase in the impervious area implies an increase in the built area (such as buildings and parking lots) and a decrease in the green spaces (such as parks and open areas). For each year, we compute the loss in green space for each CT in our analysis. We provide the summary statistics inTable 6. We observe that, on average, the green space decreases in CTs in our data. We predict that the higher the percentage of green space, the higher the chances are for interactions between people in a community and vice-versa. Table 6.Summary Statistics of Green Space Variables Table 6.Summary Statistics of Green Space Variables 3.5. Non-White Mayor Data InHypothesis 4, we build on contact theory and work in political science, arguing that authoritative figures can foster racial harmony through implicit and explicit mechanisms (Hutchings and Valentino 2004,Kinder and Dale-Riddle 2012,Tesler 2013,Chudy, Piston and Shipper 2019). For instance,Hajnal (2001)shows how Black mayors positively affect racial sentiments among the White population. To study the impact of elected minority leaders on the biased use of police, we collected information on the race of the 35 mayors for the 14 cities in our data from 2011 to 2020. We create a variable non-White Mayor, set to “1” if the mayor is not White and 0 otherwise. Five out of the 14 cities in our database had a non-White elected mayor during 2011 − 2020. 3.6. Twitter Data for Political Support Finally, to testHypothesis 5, arguing that the association between non-Black in-migration and unfounded suspicion-related calls is lower when officials express public support for Black Americans, we collect data on public support on Twitter for the Black community. First, for our analysis period, we identified mayors, elected congressional representatives, senators, and governors associated with each city each year. These politicians are arguably the most visible public figures at the city level.9We identified 112 politicians associated with the 14 cities in our data. We found the Twitter handles of 105 out of the 112 politicians in our manual search.10We collected all of the tweets authored by each of the 105 politicians from their time on Twitter from 2011 to 2020. Next, we matched each city with the corresponding mayor, elected congressional representative, two senators, and the governor in office in a year. We then used the keywords (lowercased to ensure we are able to pick up irregular capitalization in the original tweets) “black history month,” “juneteenth,” “#blm,” “black lives matter,” and “george floyd” to tag tweets that supported the Black community. We manually verified each tweet to remove the false positives. Finally, we obtained 917 verified tweets from these politicians in support of the Black community during our analysis. We then constructed a CT-year variable that captured the Black community’s public support from their elected representatives to testHypothesis 5. A summary of the hypotheses and the variables used are provided inTable 1.
article.4. Econometric Analysis and Results: 4.1. Demographic Change and Suspicion-Related Calls Our primary hypothesis argues that an increase in the non-Black population in a census tract is associated with both the percentage of (a) suspicion-related 911 calls and (b) unfounded suspicion-related calls. First, we provide some model-free evidence.Figure 4displays the predicted association between the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls and log non-Black population and the confidence interval from a fractional polynomial estimation. We observe that the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls increases as the percentage of log(non-Black population) increases.Figure 4is consistent with ourHypothesis 1. To formally test our hypotheses, we estimate a model of the form InEquation (1),iandtdenote CTs and years, respectively. The dependent variable𝑌𝑖⁢𝑡Yi⁢trefers to(1)the percentage of suspicion-related 911 calls and(2)the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls. The variables𝐿𝑜𝑔(𝑁𝑜𝑛𝐵𝑙𝑎𝑐𝑘𝑃𝑜𝑝)𝑖⁢𝑡Log(NonBlackPop)i⁢tand𝐿𝑜𝑔(𝐵𝑙𝑎𝑐𝑘𝑃𝑜𝑝)𝑖⁢𝑡Log(BlackPop)i⁢trefer to the log of the non-Black and Black populations, respectively, in a CT in a year. We also control for other factors in our model. Specifically, the variable𝐿𝑜𝑔(𝑂𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟𝐶𝑎𝑙𝑙𝑠)𝑖⁢𝑡Log(OtherCalls)i⁢tindicates the log of other 911 calls in a CT in a year, which accounts for the total crime in a CT during that period. Finally, the parameters𝛽𝑖βiand𝛽𝑡βt, respectively, refer to the CT- and year-fixed effects to control for the CT- and year-level unobserved factors on the 911 calls. InEquation (2), variable𝐻𝑖𝑔ℎ%𝐵𝑙𝑎𝑐𝑘𝑃𝑜𝑝𝐶𝑇𝑖⁢𝑡High%BlackPopCTi⁢t= 1 if the percentage of the Black population in the CTiin 2011 is above the median value of percentage of the Black population in CTs across the United States and zero otherwise. It is worth noting that in our model, the Black and non-Black populations are estimated concurrently rather than using only the percentage of the non-Black population as the right-hand-side variable. We do this to account for different sources of demographic change. For instance, the percentage of non-Black residents in an area could increase with the Black residents leaving the area. In this case, a positive and significant coefficient estimate for the percentage of the non-Black population would not indicate an increase in suspicion-related 911 calls because of the increased interracial contact—our primary theoretical claim. Second, literature in sociology and criminology has consistently found a positive association between racial bias and the Black population in a neighborhood (Quillian 1996). Therefore, an increase in the Black population in an area could influence suspicion-related 911 calls. By keeping both Black and non-Black populations as separate right-hand-side variables, we can control for the impact of the change in the Black population on suspicion-related 911 calls in the census tract. Thus, coefficient𝛽1β1in Specification 1 quantifies the percentage increase in suspicion-related 911 calls (unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls) with a one percent increase in the non-Black population in a CT while holding the Black population, total crime (captured by other 911 calls), and other unobserved census tract and time-related factors constant. And coefficients𝛽1β1and𝛽2β2in Specification 2 indicate the association between the suspicion-related 911 calls (unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls) and non-Black population in low %Black population CTs and the incremental effect on this association in high %Black population CTs, respectively. 4.2. Threats to Identification It is crucial to note that our data are observational, and endogeneity is a significant concern for interpreting our coefficients. Specifically, unobserved factors could simultaneously affect the non-Black population and the 911 calls in an area, causing bias in the coefficient for the non-Black population inEquation (1). To address this concern, we examine three categories of unobserved factors, area-specific, population-specific, and policing-specific factors, that may bias our coefficients. Below, we provide examples of these factors and discuss how we account for them in our specifications. Area-specific factors: Certain area-specific factors can increase the possibility of suspicion and hence, the suspicion-related 911 calls. For example, inner city and downtown areas in cities offer larger opportunities for interracial interactions and also have higher crime rates. Thus, it is possible to have higher suspicion-related calls in these areas than in the rural and sparsely populated areas. Policing-specific factors: Different police departments classify and handle the disposition of 911 calls differently. For example, police departments in conservative areas with a lower Black population may dispose of suspicion-related 911 calls differently than in liberal areas. Moreover, police departments receiving higher 911 calls may classify minor offenses as unfounded to show higher crime clearance rates. Population-specific factors: The social, cultural, or religious beliefs of residents in an area may influence their propensity to make 911 calls.Charles (2003)studied racial stratification of neighborhoods and found that most racial groups preferred neighbors of the same race, with White residents having the highest preference for White neighbors. Therefore, the racial composition of a neighborhood may influence suspicion-related 911 calls. It is challenging to control for these factors in our regressions because they are often not observed. We include census tract fixed effects in Specifications 1 and 2 to account for these factors that may vary across the CTs in our data. Furthermore, we include year-fixed effects to account for the change in the propensity for suspicion-related 911 calls over time. A fundamental limitation of this approach is that this set of fixed effects may not account for time-varying change across census tracts. To account for this, we use other approaches, such as matching estimators described in our robustness checks section.11Finally, to account for the correlations in CT-level unobserved factors, we cluster correct the standard errors at the census tract level. Given these econometric points, inTable 7, we report the coefficient estimates fromEquation (1)with the percentage of suspicion-related and the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls as dependent variables in Columns (1) and (3), respectively. We find positive and significant coefficients for the non-Black population in both columns, indicating an increase in the percentage of suspicion and the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls with the increase in the non-Black population in a CT, keeping the Black population, other 911 calls, and other census tract and time effects constant. These coefficients indicate a positive association between an increase in the non-Black population of a census tract and the rise in unfounded suspicious calls. Columns (2) and (4) show coefficient estimates for the percentage of suspicion-related and the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls fromEquation (2). The insignificant coefficients for the non-Black population in these columns indicate the lack of association between the percentage of suspicion and the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls with non-Black populations in low %Black population CTs. Moreover, the sum of NonBlackPop and NonBlackPop × High%BlackPopCT coefficients in columns (2) and (4) is positive and significant, indicating a higher percentage of (unfounded) suspicion-related 911 calls with the increase in non-Black population in high %Black population CTs. Higher percentage of (unfounded) suspicion-related 911 calls with the increase in the non-Black population in Black resident majority CTs indicates the biased use of police and thus supportsHypothesis 1. Thus, whereas suspicion-related 911 calls positively correlate with an increasing non-Black population, they are not associated with the increase in the Black population. 4.3. Heterogeneous Effects of Demographic Change 4.3.1. Gentrifiable Census Tracts. As discussed in our theory section and literature review, prior theory suggests that not all census tracts should be equally affected by an increase in the non-Black population. We use literature to classify tracts as “gentrifiable.” As before, we provide some model-free evidence.Figure 5is similar toFigure 4, but it displays the fitted association between the percentage of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls and the log of non-Black population across gentrifiable and non-gentrifiable tracts from a fractional polynomial estimation. FollowingFreeman (2005), we consider a CT gentrifiable if it (1) belongs to the inner city and (2) has a low investment in housing stock post-1980. But we find positive and significant coefficients for the interaction terms in all cases, indicating that the increase in the non-Black population in gentrifiable CTs has a significantly higher positive correlation to suspicion-related and unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls than other CTs. These results indicate a stronger relationship between an increase in the non-Black population and a rise in unfounded suspicion calls in gentrifiable areas. As discussed in the previous section (Table 5), gentrifiable CTs have a considerably higher proportion of low-income Black households with no college degree. Our theory suggests that interracial—and inter-status—contact in these communities may be one reason for the rise in unfounded suspicion calls. In contrast, other CTs have a predominantly White population. Movement of non-Black residents to such areas does not increase interracial contact; perhaps, for this reason, we do not see an increase in suspicion-related 911 calls there. Together, the results fromTables 8and9supportHypothesis 2. 4.4. Robustness Checks So far, our findings indicate that when non-Black individuals migrate into gentrifiable census tracts (CTs), there is an increase in the proportion of unfounded suspicion-related calls, which serves as a measure of biased police utilization. However, this increase is not observed in other CTs. The non-Black population, upon encountering a pre-existing Black population in gentrifiable CTs, is theorized to experience a sense of threat in line with racial threat theory. This finding suggests that the biased use of police by the non-Black population against Black community members is driven by perceptions of crime rather than actual criminal activity. However, it is possible that unobserved differences between the gentrifiable and non-gentrifiable CTs—besides the levels of interracial contact—could cause a rise in unfounded suspicion calls against the Black residents, even after controlling for crime. To test this possibility, we account for the differences in socioeconomic characteristics between the gentrifiable and non-gentrifiable CTs with several matching estimators (Iacus et al. 2012). Our objective is to identify CTs similar to the gentrifiable CTs in the number, income, and education of the existing non-Black population in 2011. Moreover, during our analysis time frame, these CTs experienced similar growth in income, education, and housing stocks as gentrifiable CTs. Accordingly, we matched the CTs in our data on the following variables: Socioeconomic variables in 2011: • Non-Black population • Number of low/medium/high-income non-Black households • Number of non-Black residents 25 years and above with and without a college degree • Median housing values Change in socioeconomic variables from 2011 to 2020 • Change in the college-educated population • Change in median housing value • Number of new housing units built • Number of households moving into new housing units We match the gentrifiable CTs with non-gentrifiable CTs using coarsened exact matching (CEM) estimators (Iacus et al. 2012). We divided each variable into bins of different sizes. We consider two CTs matched if they fall in the same bin for all variables. We coarsened the variables to different levels (divided them into different numbers of bins) to check whether our estimates were sensitive to the extent of matching between the two categories of CTs. Specifically, we divided the matching variables into two, four, six, eight, and 10 bins. We estimate Specification 3 on data for matched CTs based on different levels of variable coarsening. Table 10reports the coefficient estimates for the whole and matched data at different levels of variable coarsening. We find that matching at more granular levels reduces the number of matched CTs (i.e., matching at smaller-sized variable bins). Across different levels of coarsening, we find insignificant non-Black coefficients in all cases, which indicates an absence of significant relation of the non-Black population with the suspicion-related 911 calls for non-gentrifiable CTs. But we find positive and significant coefficients for the interaction terms in all cases, indicating that the increase in the non-Black population in gentrifiable CTs has a significantly higher positive relationship with suspicion-related and unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls than other CTs. Does Contact Between High- and Low-Income Residents Increase Biased Use of Police? Another possible confounder is that high-income individuals perceive a more significant threat from low-income residents, regardless of race. We conduct the following two falsification tests to rule out this alternative explanation for our results. Does in-migration of the non-Black population in non-gentrifiable CTs result in higher suspicion-related 911 calls? Suppose non-Black residents view their low-income Black counterparts with suspicion for economic reasons. In that case, we should see more suspicion-related 911 calls when they move into non-gentrifiable CTs with existing low-income non-Black populations. Table 5reveals a similar average value of total low-income households (annual income less than $50,000) in the gentrifiable and non-gentrifiable CTs (761 and 673). However, whereas low-income Black households account for a significant proportion of low-income households in gentrifiable CTs (273/761), low-income non-Black households comprise the majority of low-income households in non-gentrifiable CTs (565/674). Suppose the incoming non-Black population is moving to a community with a predominantly low-income non-Black population in non-gentrifiable CTs. If we believe that the high-suspicion-related 911 calls are due to the general threat perception about low-income neighbors among high-income people, then we should see an increase in suspicion-related 911 calls with the increase in the non-Black population in non-gentrifiable CTs. We test this fact withEquation (3)and report the estimated coefficients inTable 11below. Does Increased Contact Between Members of Different Races Increase Biased Use of Police? Racial threat theory posits that White residents feel threatened when they come in contact with Black residents in a neighborhood. Studies on the psychology of racism (Bobo and Massagli 2001) discuss that racial stereotyping is implicit and unconscious. Such unconscious bias may be triggered by contact with members of other races. In the previous section, we argued that the movement of non-Black individuals into gentrifiable CTs with a sizeable Black population increases the likelihood of interracial contact. However, a high Black population in a neighborhood does not necessarily imply high interracial contact because different races could self-segregate in micro-geographies within a CT (Bickford and Massey 1991,Tach 2014,Chaskin and Joseph 2015). The availability of green space in a CT provides a measure of contact between residents of different communities. Therefore, CTs with a lower decrease (higher increase) in green space offer higher opportunities for racial intermingling and thus could increase the suspicion-related 911 calls. We test this reasoning by estimating the following econometric specification: All variables have the same meaning as in Specification 3. The variableIncGreenSpaceindicates the increase in the percentage of green space in a CT from 2011 to 2020. The interaction coefficient𝛽2β2captures the marginal effect of an increase in the non-Black population in CTs with the increase in percentage of green space (i.e., high opportunities for intermingling). A positive and significant estimate for𝛽2β2would mean that movement of the non-Black population in neighborhoods with higher opportunities for interracial contact is associated with higher suspicion-related 911 calls. Table 13reports the coefficient from Specification 4 on gentrifiable and non-gentrifiable CTs separately. We find a positive and significant interaction coefficient for the percentage of suspicion-related and unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls in the case of gentrifiable CTs but not in others. This finding suggests an increase in biased use of police with higher opportunities for interracial contact, thus supportingHypothesis 3. The increased interaction between different races has several consequences. Our results suggest that increased green spaces in communities, particularly those with historical disinvestment, may have unintended consequences. Our findings are consistent with media articles that discuss how members of the Black community are routinely discriminated against in public spaces, particularly in parks (Mock 2016,Griggs 2018). In fact,Sigelman and Welch (1993)discussed how living environments that stimulate interracial contact do not promote racial integration. Interestingly, the variation in green spaces in non-gentrifiable CTs, with a low possibility of racial intermingling, does not affect the suspicion-related 911 calls with the increase in the non-Black population. 4.6. The findings on this dimension are promising but mixed. For instance,Hajnal (2001)showed how under Black mayors, racial sentiments can improve among the members of the White community. However, a single politician may not sway deeply ingrained prejudice (Rothbart and John 1993,Fazio et al. 1995,Fiske 1998). The coefficient for the mayor of color is positive and significant, indicating higher suspicion-related calls in areas with a mayor of color. This result could be because areas with a high Black population are more likely to elect a person of color as mayor, and such neighborhoods could have higher racial bias (Quillian and Pager 2001). Interestingly, we find a negative and significant coefficient for the interaction term, indicating a negative association between unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls and the non-Black population in areas with non-White mayors. This result suggests that non-White representation in politics can significantly impact racial prejudice.Hajnal (2001)called this the “informational effect” and how the election of Black mayors can help the White community observe Black authorities’ actions firsthand instead of relying on racial stereotyping or political rhetoric. Effect of the Call for Racial Harmony from the Authorities on Racial Bias Another factor influencing interracial contact is the explicit support of authorities for racial harmony (Allport et al. 1954,Powers and Ellison 1995,Hood and Morris 1998,Pettigrew and Tropp 2005). As discussed in the data section, we first obtained the names of the mayors of each city, congressional representatives, two senators, and governors of the state in which each city is located for each year from 2011 to 2020. We then obtained their Twitter handles and all the tweets these elected representatives wrote. We identify the tweets supporting the Black community using Juneteenth, Black Lives Matter, and Black History month keywords and hashtags. We estimate the following specification: We present the results inTable 15. Consistent with our main results inTable 7, we find that the increase in the non-Black population in a neighborhood is associated with an increase in unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls. We also find that an increase in tweets supporting the Black community by officials (controlling for the overall activity on Twitter by these elected officials) also increases unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls. This might be because people in authority may tweet more to support racial harmony during times of racial conflict. However, we find a negative and significant coefficient for the interaction term. This result suggests that communities that experience an increase in the non-Black population and an increase in the number of tweets from elected officials supporting the Black community are likely to experience a decrease in unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls. Thus, we find support forHypothesis 5. We acknowledge that the number of tweets supporting the Black community is endogenous in our Specification 6. Our coefficient shows an interesting correlation that provides a potential opportunity to use digital mechanisms to mitigate racial prejudice. Our results are thus consistent with the predictions from contact theory; authoritative support and institutional backing play a role in decreasing racial prejudice when members of different races come together. Thus, we find support for ourHypothesis 5. Our results highlight how social media can be used as a valuable tool by elected authorities to influence interracial relations positively. Authoritative figures can leverage social media tools to provide institutional backing to efforts promoting racial harmony. Together, our results suggest that interracial contact can increase racial prejudice, especially when the racial groups have inequality in education and income levels. However, we find that interracial contact reduces prejudice when communities have non-White mayors and political leaders express support for the Black community on social media platforms.
article.5. Discussion: Our work holds implications for research and policy. On the one hand, scholars have documented increased political polarization and rising racial tensions in recent years. Despite advances in civil rights and racial attitudes over the last 60 years, racial distrust remains a critical facet of life in America. Although this distrust has become less overt, there is broad agreement in the literature that it persists in subtle, often unconscious, yet systemic ways (Bobo and Massagli 2001,De Houwer et al. 2009,O’Brien et al. 2010). This paper studies one mechanism through which this distrust can profoundly affect communities. Specifically, as the racial composition of once predominantly Black neighborhoods changes—as non-Black residents move in—we observe an increase in suspicion as measured by the number of unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls to police. This effect is most significant in communities experiencing gentrification and where interracial interaction is facilitated—through the growth of public green spaces. However, there are bright spots; in communities with non-White leadership and ones where political representatives—of any race—express support for Black lives, the biased use of police appears weaker. Our results have practical implications for lawmakers and city administrators as well. Although the increase in suspicion-related 911 calls is small in terms of percentage, the additional costs to the communities because of such unwarranted calls are nontrivial. Such unwarranted 911 calls take scarce police resources away from stemming actual crimes and thus inflict indirect costs of additional crime to society. We interviewed a lawmaker from San Francisco who was instrumental in passing a law that penalizes racially motivated and false reporting of 911 calls.13He emphasized that such calls are made within a social contract; police officers and 911 call dispatchers implicitly trust the person making the call to provide the correct information. As a result, dispatched police officers operate with this information until arriving at the scene. He stressed that false and racially motivated 911 calls violate this implicit contract between law enforcement and the public. Finally, and perhaps more importantly, the unfounded suspicious calls impose psychological and emotional stress on the Black residents because of the trauma of being stereotyped and reported unnecessarily. This issue does not just create psychological costs in the short term. It may lead to more long-term trauma and other costs (e.g., having to leave the neighborhood because of harassment). Victims of biased policing in the community report how this makes them feel unsafe performing everyday tasks that a White person would not be afraid to do. We interviewed the Vice President DEI of a large midwestern university. She holds a PhD in Communications and is an African American woman and is also a victim of the biased use of police by neighbors.14She is a passionate athlete and mentioned how she always has her business card on her when she goes running “just in case” she gets stopped by the police again and the interaction becomes fatal. Furthermore, she talked about how running in a neighborhood, even as a prolific athlete, can cause discomfort among her neighbors. Her experience is not an isolated incident and is consistent with anecdotal evidence from Black Americans. ANew York Timesarticle titled “Running While Black: Our Readers Respond” documents several such incidents.15Indeed, studies in psychology have documented the emotional stress, anger, resentment, and disappointment of being stereotyped and constantly placed under higher surveillance among Black individuals relative to their non-Black counterparts. Researchers call this stress “racial battle fatigue” (Smith et al. 2007). Beyond our contribution to the literature on racial bias, we speak to several streams of literature in the social sciences and information systems. One contribution of this paper is to provide quantitative evidence—albeit correlational and tentative—of a systemic bias in the use of police as neighborhood demographics change. Although this mechanism has been discussed widely in the popular press and anecdotal evidence has accumulated, rigorous examination of this phenomenon is challenging. Our novel data set of more than 39 million 911 calls allows us to measure and study this phenomenon systematically. Furthermore, we provide tests of two critical theories in this domain—contact theory and racial threat theory—with observational data. In contrast, most prior work in this area has relied on self-reported surveys or simulated laboratory experiments (Dasgupta and Greenwald 2001,Quillian and Pager 2001,Schmidt and Nosek 2010,Columb and Plant 2016,Lai et al. 2016,Schmidt and Axt 2016). Another contribution of our work is to research in information systems on the value of public data to improve transparency in governance. Our work shows that these data—on the activities of the police in major American cities—can help measure implicit bias at scale. These fine-grained data are only possible because governments implement technology to improve transparency at the level of local governments. This effort requires using computer-aided dispatch systems, collecting data in real-time from the officer dispatched to the scene, anonymizing and updating these records, and releasing such large-scale data to the public. Our study shows that “Ecometrics” (Raudenbush and Sampson 1999) can provide insight into systemic challenges faced by communities. Moreover, we distinguish between these predictions using data on community leadership and social media. Although the overall patterns of our results support racial threat theory, we see evidence of the more subtle mechanisms from contact theory, namely, that authority figures and explicit institutional support for racial harmony lead to a weakening of biased use of police. In testing this mechanism, we contribute to the small but growing body of literature in Information Systems, studying the impact of online activism in Americans' everyday lives. For example,Agarwal and Sen (2021)studied the impact of the #BlackLivesMatter movement on demand for antiracist curriculum, whereas other studies have shown how the #MeToo movement affected hiring decisions (Luo and Zhang 2021) and sexual harassment complaints (Levy and Mattsson 2019). Finally, we contribute to the emergent IS, Marketing, and Operations Management literature that studies inequality in different contexts and interventions to mitigate them (Edelman et al. 2017,Mayya et al. 2021,Zhang et al. 2021,Cui et al. 2022,Mitkina et al. 2022). Specifically,Cui et al. (2020)andCui et al. (2021)discussed interventions that can mitigate racial bias on online platforms. Our work shows how community leaders’ digital support lessens gentrification’s adverse effects. Our study has limitations, and readers should interpret our results with these caveats in mind. Three important ones are worth highlighting specifically. While a critical strength of our study relative to prior work is that we use observational data on actual 911 calls and their outcomes. These data allow us to observe evidence of the biased use of police in the real world at a larger scale than in past work. However, they limit our ability to make causal claims; we do not have an independent and exogenous shock to demographic trends in census tracts that would allow us to estimate our mechanism of interest better. We work to reduce the possibility of spuriousness in various ways by estimating fixed-effect models and matching estimators. Our results remain consistent across various specifications, giving us greater confidence, although not certainty. Future research should focus on better estimating the causal impact of this phenomenon on the frequency of unfounded calls on other outcomes that are relevant in this context, for example, with measures of the costs to citizens of such behaviors. Furthermore, we estimate our models with data from 14 cities where these detailed data were available. There is undoubtedly unobserved heterogeneity in which cities make data on 911 calls and police activity available to the public. However, this heterogeneity would likely lead to a downward bias in our estimates because cities with more significant bias problems are perhaps less likely to make such data available. Finally, although quite detailed, our data do not include information about the caller or the individuals on whom they called the police. Consequently, we must infer the direction of racial bias from aggregate data, a challenge in any empirical context. However, across various specifications and estimates, the most consistent evidence suggests that unfounded suspicion-related calls rise as non-Black residents move into predominantly Black communities and almost under no other circumstance. Although we acknowledge that our study’s ability to make strong causal claims is limited, our analysis still offers several insights for policy. First, we document systematic variation in unfounded suspicion-related 911 calls in rapidly gentrifying neighborhoods. Although one positive element of our analysis is that police officers do indeed ultimately classify some of these calls as “unfounded,” they impose both economic and psychological costs on the targets of these calls. Policymakers should consider mechanisms that deter biased 911 calls, for instance, potential fines—perhaps significant—for callers who frequently make unfounded calls. Another possibility is to make such call data public and more visible. The public nature of these data may serve as a deterrent for individuals who inappropriately make such calls.
